Position asymmetry.

Hila are vascular with appropriate terminal branching.
Hemidiaphragms are appropriately positioned, right costo-phrenic sinus clear, left shaded - pleural component.

Atheromatosis of the aortic arch.

Due to the orientation finding of moderate mitral regurgitation and mild aortic regurgitation, an outpatient cardiology consultation is advised (perform transthoracic heart ultrasound), perform 24-hour Holter ECG, followed by a cardiologist review with findings.

No significant past illnesses. First cardiology consultation in November 2020 due to occasional rapid and irregular heartbeat. Symptoms used to occur once a month with a frequency of around 130-150 beats per minute lasting an hour. Holter monitoring and ergometry were done, but the arrhythmia was never captured.
Current Illness: For the past 10 days, experiencing rapid and irregular heartbeat even with minimal physical activity. Reportedly normofrequent only when lying down. Denies chest pain and shortness of breath.
Functions: Normal stool and urination.
Habits: Smokes more than one pack per day, does not drink alcohol.
Chronic Medications: None
Drug Allergies: None
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build and nourishment, conscious, alert, eupneic at rest, mobile, afebrile.
Skin and visible mucous membranes are well perfused. Peripheral lymph nodes not palpable.
Neck: Symmetrical, mobile, thyroid not enlarged on palpation. Neck veins properly filled. No vascular bruits.
Chest: Properly shaped, symmetrical, with even respiratory movements.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds on auscultation.
Heart: Regular rhythm, muffled tones, no murmurs, BP 110/80 mmHg. SpO2 97%.
Abdomen: Level with the chest, soft, elastic, non-tender on palpation, liver and spleen not palpable. Audible peristalsis. Lumbar percussion painless.
Extremities: Symmetrical, no edema, palpable peripheral pulses.
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 157/min, physiological electrical axis, no changes in the final oscillation.
Control ECG (12:35h): Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 92/min, physiological electrical axis, no changes in the final oscillation.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs, 1 Direction
Summary image of thoracic organs, PA and lateral views:
Marked bilateral interstitial markings with cranial redistribution of blood vessels and vascular congestion in the hila, indicating congestion changes.
Condensed pulmonary parenchyma seen retrocardially on the right basal and lateral view—prolonged inflammatory infiltration of the parenchyma cannot be excluded. Follow-up needed.
Right hemidiaphragm appropriately positioned, left flattened, and shallower costo-phrenic sinuses—pleural component.

Calcified aortic arch.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.3 [1e9]/L, RBC 5.00 [1e12]/L, HGB 155 g/L, Hct 0.479 L/L, MCV 95.8 fL, MCH 31.0 pg, MCHC 324 g/L, RDW-CV 13.4 %, Platelets 103 [1e9]/L, MPV 13.5 fL, NEU% 64.7 %, LYM% 23.2 %, MONO% 9.4 %, EOS% 2.1 %, BASO% 0.6 %, NEU # 4.06 [1e9]/L, LYM # 1.46 [1e9]/L, MONO # 0.59 [1e9]/L, EOS # 0.13 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.04 [1e9]/L, NRBC/100 0 /100 leukocytes, PV-share 0.99, APTT 22.7 s, APTT R 0.85, Blood Glucose 7.6 mmol/L, Urea 7.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 79 µmol/L, eGFR 65.6 mL/min/1.73m², Total Bilirubin 17 µmol/L, Direct Bilirubin 6 µmol/L, Indirect Bilirubin 11 µmol/L, AST 53 U/L, ALT 81 U/L, GGT 38 U/L, LDH 247 U/L, ALP 93 U/L, Total Protein 65 g/L, Albumin 37.8 g/L, CRP 9.9 mg/L, Na 142 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, Cl 110 mmol/L

Transfusion Therapy
No indication for urgent hospitalization in the Internal Medicine Clinic at this time. An echocardiogram and 24-hour Holter ECG monitoring are advised. Follow-up with a cardiologist for results and to decide on the need for anticoagulant therapy as per cardiologist's recommendation. Given the high risk of thromboembolic events, it is advised to initiate anticoagulant therapy—Martefarin or NOAC as agreed with the patient and the chosen family physician. Regular control of TSH, lipid profile, and blood pressure monitoring. Dabigatran Viatris 150 mg 2x1.
Conclusion
Senior service consulted. The evaluation showed no indication for urgent hospitalization. Due to the unknown duration of atrial fibrillation, conversion to sinus rhythm was not performed, and only rate control was recommended. Given the low thromboembolic risk (CHADs-VASc: 1, HAS-BLED: 1), it is advised to take Concor 5 mg 1x1 (if heart rate exceeds 110/min, increase dose to a maximum of 10 mg with blood pressure monitoring) for at least 4 weeks, after which a referral to the Cardiology Day Hospital (no. 557-253) for potential electrocardioversion is recommended.
Considering the frequency and symptoms of the arrhythmia, a consultation with an arrhythmologist for possible ablation treatment is advised.
Due to clinical signs of cough and RTG changes, it is recommended to start antibiotic therapy with Klavocin bid 2x1 for a week. Follow-up lab results after completing antimicrobial therapy. Control chest and heart X-rays in 3-4 weeks.
In case of worsening, report to the relevant emergency service!

In January 2019, he was hospitalized in cardiology due to breathing difficulties and elevated cardio-selective enzymes due to acute pneumonia and heart failure with a minor myocardial infarction. Invasive diagnostics confirmed patent bypass grafts with multiple stenotic-occlusive changes in native coronary arteries. Echocardiography showed significantly impaired left ventricular systolic function due to ischemic cardiomyopathy. During the same hospitalization, due to pain in the left groin, an abdominal MSCT was performed, confirming a hematoma in the left psoas muscle (which was later monitored and regressed). He occasionally has episodes of atrial fibrillation and is on anticoagulant therapy. Due to ischemic cardiomyopathy with severe systolic dysfunction despite optimal revascularization and medication therapy, ICD implantation was indicated. A single-chamber implantable cardioverter defibrillator (Medtronic D364VRM, Protecta VR) was implanted for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death. According to the latest BIS report, stimulation parameters before discharge, as well as morphology discrimination, are normal; the device is programmed in VVI mode LRL 40/min with detection in VT (171-207/min) and VF (>207/min) zones.

It is advised to take Ciprinol 500 mg 2x1 tablet at home with Medazol 400 mg 3x1 tablet for 10 days, Controloc 40 mg 2x1 tablet for 7 days (then 1x1 tablet in the morning on an empty stomach), Buscopan 10 mg 1-2 tablets 3-5 times a day, following a colitis diet.
Control of laboratory findings (CBC, differential, creatinine, Na, K, CRP, AST, ALT, GGT, LDH, ALP, bilirubins) in 2 days with the primary care physician. Adequate hydration is recommended (Rehydromix solution can be used) along with a probiotic.

Follow-up with a gastroenterologist, an internal referral issued.
Colonoscopy is advised in two months.
Control with the primary care physician.
In case of symptom worsening, contact the nearest emergency medical service and/or Emergency Department.

History
Personal Medical History:

Denies any trauma.
Took Klavocin for a week (from Tuesday to the day before admission) without improvement.
No chest pain, dyspnea, chills, or shivering reported.
Denies palpitations or insect bites.
Functions:
Good appetite, no weight fluctuations.
Regular bowel movements with normal consistency and no pathological additions.
Normal urination without dysuria; urine is clear.
Habits:
Occasional smoker, does not consume alcohol.
Current Medications:
None.
Allergies:
Denies any drug or environmental allergies.

Examination Findings
General Appearance: Medium build, alert, mobile, communicative, oriented, eupneic, afebrile, and non-icteric.

Flat, soft, elastic, non-tender.

Mobile.

Right foot dorsum shows pinpoint rash that does not blanch on pressure.
Homan’s test negative.
ECG: Sinus rhythm, heart rate 79/min, physiological electrical axis, no ST-segment abnormalities.

Diagnostics and Consultations
Laboratory Findings:
WBC 8.4 [1e9]/L, Erc 4.16 [1e12]/L, HGB 134 g/L, Hct 0.406 L/L, MCV 97.6 fL, MCH 32.2 pg, MCHC 330 g/L, RDW-CV 13.1 %, Trc 242 [1e9]/L, MPV 10.8 fL, NEU% 59.8 %, LYM% 25.4 %, MONO% 13.0 %, EOS% 1.4 %, BASO% 0.4 %, NEU # 5.03 [1e9]/L, LYM # 2.14 [1e9]/L, MONO # 1.09 [1e9]/L, EOS# 0.12 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.03 [1e9]/L, NRBC/100 0 /100 Lkc, PV-udjel 1.11 , APTV 22.1 s, APTV R 0.82 , DD 2.11 mg/L , GUK S 5.2 mmol/L, UREJA 4.3 mmol/L, KREA 74 µmol/L, eGFR 125.3 mL/min/1.73m2, AST 28 U/L, ALT 20 U/L, GGT 16 U/L, LDH 179 U/L, ALP 101 U/L, CRP 3.8 mg/L, Na 142 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, Cl - S 105 mmol/L

Left common femoral vein (VFC), superficial femoral vein (VFS), and popliteal vein (VP): compressible, patent, and without signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
All three pairs of deep calf veins (tibial posterior, fibular, and anterior tibial) are compressible and patent, with no signs of DVT.

Provisional Diagnosis: Likely cellulitis of the left lower leg. DVT excluded via ultrasound.
Treatment Plan:
Continue Klavocin 2 × 1 g daily for 7 more days.
Apply cold compresses to the affected area.
For pain: Brufen 600 mg 2 × 1 daily with Controloc 20 mg 1 × 1 daily.
Follow-Up:
Routine laboratory tests upon completing antibiotic therapy.
Regular follow-up with the primary care physician.
Emergency Instructions:
Return immediately to the Emergency Infectious Diseases Department if condition worsens, redness spreads, or fever develops.

Hypoechogenic region in the upper third of the right lobe: Indicative of lymphocytic thyroiditis.

Total thyroidectomy with pre- and paratracheal neck dissection performed at [Hospital] (ENT Clinic).

Left Lobe: Papillary carcinoma, 2.4x1.7x1.3 cm (classic type), with lymphovascular invasion, no extrathyroidal spread.

Hospitalized in June 2023 in Infectious Diseases due to urosepsis after lithotripsy (UK: P. aeruginosa). Emergency esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGDS) performed due to vomiting of blood-stained content, revealing two erosions at the gastric fundus and bulbar erosions. ECG showed atrial flutter, leading to electrical cardioversion on September 27, achieving stable sinus rhythm. **Triage Data:**
Triage Category: 2; Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): 0;
**Vital Signs**
**Medical History:**
- **Personal History and Past Illnesses:** The patient suffered a myocardial infarction (MI) 20 years ago and had one stent placed. He was hospitalized in the Cardiology Department in 2020 due to angina. An invasive cardiology examination verified borderline changes in the midsection of the left anterior descending artery (LAD) and circumflex artery (Cx), as well as subtotal stenosis of the distal right coronary artery (RCA). During the same procedure, a percutaneous coronary intervention was performed on the right coronary artery with the successful placement of a drug-eluting stent.
- **Current Illness:** Today, while working in the field, the patient experienced chest pain and shortness of breath, followed by loss of consciousness. He presented to the emergency medical services, where an ECG showed ventricular tachycardia (VT). He was treated with Cordarone 300 mg in 250 ml of 5% glucose intravenously.
- **Habits:** Does not smoke, does not consume alcohol.
- **Therapy:** Cardiopirin, hypolipidemic agent.
- **Allergies:** Denies any drug allergies.
**Status:**
The patient is conscious, communicative, and oriented in time and space, but experiencing tachypnea. Skin and visible mucous membranes are well perfused. Lungs: auscultation reveals normal bilateral breath sounds, SpO2 at 99%. Heart: rapid action, clear tones, no murmurs heard, blood pressure 113/72 mmHg. Abdomen: level with the chest, soft, non-tender on palpation, audible peristalsis. Limbs: no edema.
**ECG:** Ventricular tachycardia with a frequency of approximately 200 beats per minute.
**Diagnostic and Consultative Tests:**
Laboratory results are pending.
**Control Measurements:**
- Blood Pressure: 125/54 mmHg
- SpO2: 94%
- Control ECG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 66 beats per minute, physiological electrical axis, left bundle branch block (LBBB) from previous records.

- Hypnomidate 20 mg intravenously
- Reglan ampoule I in 100 ml of fluid intravenously
- Oxygen 6L/min via mask
- Atropine II ampoule intravenously

In shallow analgesia and sedation with etomidate, synchronized cardioversion (200J) was performed, resulting in conversion to a stable sinus rhythm. The patient is conscious, in verbal contact, hemodynamically stable, with SpO2 at 96%.
Patient discharged with recommendation to come back if worsened.

In sections through the lung bases, a right pleural effusion 1.5 cm wide is shown.
No signs of pathological bone remodeling are seen.

Acute pancreatitis CTSI 2-3.

Abdominal Ultrasound
Gallbladder with normal wall thickness, suspicious for sludge.
Pancreas mildly edematous, hyperechoic, without zones of necrosis or formed collections, a thin layer of free fluid peripancreatically. No ductal dilatation. Prominent and tortuous initial part of the pancreatic duct.
Perform fecal elastase, MR of the LS spine, physiatrist/neurologist examination p.r.n.
GE follow-up with fresh labs and control abdominal ultrasound (fasting) in one month 07.11.2025. at 08:00 ([Hospital], 3rd floor).

Conclusion:
Mr. [Patient] is a patient between 65 and 70 years of age who is hospitalized at the Department of Gastroenterology due to epigastric pain.
Initial laboratory and radiological workup confirmed acute pancreatitis.
On the ward, he was treated with ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, pantoprazole, hyoscine butylbromide, furosemide, amlodipine, potassium citrate, ipratropium bromide, dutasteride tamsulosin, diclofenac, paracetamol, low molecular weight heparin, enteral preparations, and crystalloid solutions. The administered therapy resulted in clinical and subjective improvement.
He is discharged to home care in stable condition with the recommendation to take the following therapy:
- ciprofloxacin 500mg, 2x1, for 5 days
- metronidazole 200 mg, 3x1, for 5 days
- pantoprazol 40mg, 1x1
- Vital 2x1 (NRS >3)
- DusterDuo 1x1
- DIETARY MEASURES!
Control blood count via GP in one week.
Perform fecal elastase, MR of the LS spine, physiatrist/neurologist examination p.r.n.
GE follow-up with fresh labs and control abdominal ultrasound (fasting) in one month 07.11.2025. at 08:00 ([Hospital], 3rd floor).

In case of complaints, report to the ED (OHBP).

NOTE: For every new examination, please bring this finding.
Instead of the prescribed medicine, the chosen primary health care doctor can prescribe another medicine of the same efficiency class.

Triage Category: 4

Diagnoses
R10 Abdominal and pelvic pain

Triage Data
Triage cat: 4; BP: 140/90 mmHg; Pulse: 110/min; SpO2: 96%; VAS: 3-Mild pain; GCS: 15;

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Abdominal pain

Anamnesis
PRESENT ILLNESS: Since this afternoon, has had dull pain in the LLQ, vomited stomach contents 3x, denies fever, dyspnea, and chest pain.
PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
The patient is between 65 and 70 years old and is being treated for Crohn's disease; last gastroenterology check-up 3/2025 - colonoscopy planned. In 2/2022, treated with mechanical ventilation due to pneumonia as part of a Covid-19 infection. Follow-up examination by a pulmonologist in 08/2022 with no further pulmonology follow-up required. Examined in 05/2024 at the Emergency Hospital Department (OHBP) due to high blood pressure values. Due to albuminuria, examined by a nephrologist; MSCT urography performed. Cholecystectomy performed in 12/2024. Known arterial hypertension, gout, and hyperlipidemia. Examined in 5/2025 at the emergency department due to abdominal pain. 2025 EGDS: Duodenal bulb ulcers (Forrest III). Previously known CKD, cystically changed left kidney. Appendectomy performed in 08/2025.
CURRENT THERAPY: Sulfasalazine 500 2x1, Allopurinol 100 mg 1x1 tab, Coupet 20 mg 1x1, Controloc 40 mg 1x1, Exforge 10/160 1x1, Letizen 10 mg 1x1
DRUG ALLERGIES: Denies
FUNCTIONS: Stool is normocholuric, without pathological additions, last one this morning. Urination is normal without dysuric symptoms. Appetite is weakened, no weight loss.
HABITS: Does not smoke, does not drink alcohol.

STATUS
Conscious, oriented, mobile, eupneic at rest, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes have normal perfusion. Lymph nodes not palpable.
Lungs: normal breath sounds on auscultation.
Heart: rhythmic heart action, clear tones, no murmurs heard.
Abdomen: at chest level, slightly tense, painful on palpation in the LLQ, bowel sounds audible, liver and spleen not palpable.
Lumbar succussion (LS) negative bilaterally.
Extremities: symmetrical without edema.
___________
ECG: sinus rhythm, rate 93/min, physiological heart axis, inverted T waves in aVR, V2, V1.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 23:05
Findings and Opinion
Emergency MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, native and with i.v. contrast:
Dilatation of small bowel loops is visible, lumen width up to 4 cm, edematous walls, with clear visualization of the transition point paramedially left, at the level of the iliac crest - mechanical obstruction due to adhesion - chronic appendicitis.
A small amount of free fluid is visible interintestinally.
Colon with collapsed lumen, no haustrations.
Cholecystectomized.
Hydronephrosis of the left kidney, grade V.
Other findings unremarkable.

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 21:01
Findings and Opinion
Plain radiograph of thoracic organs, PA and left profile:
In the lung parenchyma available for analysis, no convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes are seen.
Hila are vascular, with normal terminal branching.
Diaphragmatic domes and associated costophrenic (f.c.) angles are free.
Cardiac shadow is enlarged.
Plain abdominal X-ray:
On the plain abdominal X-ray, no free gas is seen under the diaphragmatic dome.
Several small air-fluid levels of the small bowel are visible.

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 20:43
HEMATOLOGY: WBC 7.0 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.76 [1e12]/L, HGB 137 g/L, Hct 0.428 L/L, MCV 89.9 fL, MCH 28.8 pg, MCHC 320 g/L, RDW-CV 15.1 %, Plt 210 [1e9]/L, MPV 9.2 fL. HEMATOLOGY: NEU% 76.2 %, LYM% 16.8 %, MONO% 6.3 %, EOS% 0.4 %, BASO% 0.3 %, NEU # 5.32 [1e9]/L, LYM # 1.17 [1e9]/L, MONO # 0.44 [1e9]/L, EOS# 0.03 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.02 [1e9]/L. HEMATOLOGY: NRBC/100 0 /100 Lkc. COAGULATION: PT-share 1.21, INR 0.92, APTT 23.6 s, APTT R 0.94. PHYSICAL-CHEMICAL URINE EXAMINATION: APPEARANCE turbid, COLOR yellow, pH 5.5, SP. GRAVITY 1.023 kg/L, GLU neg., BIL neg., KETONES neg., Hb trace, PROT 4+, UBG norm., NITRITES neg., LEU E 1+. PHYSICAL-CHEMICAL URINE EXAMINATION: Urine Leuk. 10 /high power field, Urine RBC 3 /high power field, Squamous epith. few /high power field, Hyaline casts rare /high power field, Bacteria some /high power field, Mucus some /high power field, Granular casts 1 on the entire slide, Fine granular casts 2 on the entire slide. BIOCHEMISTRY: Glucose S 7.8 mmol/L, UREA 6.5 mmol/L, CREA 107 μmol/L, eGFR 46.4 mL/min/1.73m2, T-BIL 8 μmol/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: AST 20 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, GGT 36 U/L, LDH 161 U/L, ALP 93 U/L, AMS-s 51 U/L, Lipase 33 U/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: CRP 4.4 mg/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl - S 106 mmol/L, Ca 2.37 mmol/L.

Therapy
Tramal 50 ml iv in 100 Fo. Buscopan amp iv

Conclusion
Refer to surgeon, admission to surgery.

Subsequently received adjuvant CT (chemotherapy) according to the CAPEOX protocol (5 cycles, 16.03.18 – 20.06.18). Last oncology check-up in 09/2025 – albuminuria and proteinuria, follow-up in one year.
Current complaints: For the past week, has been feeling slight pain/discomfort in the upper quadrants bilaterally with propagation to the back. The pain is constant, of equal intensity. Denies nausea and vomiting. Has been febrile up to 38°C for the last 3 days. GP prescribed Citeral 500 mg, which she has been taking since 24.10. (took the last tablet this morning). This morning, a rapid antigen test (BAT) for Covid and Influenza was performed – neg.

report_content:
**Diagnoses**
**J22 Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection**

**Anamnesis:**
**Reason for visit:** Initial examination by a pulmonologist.
**Personal history and past illnesses:** The patient, between 55 and 60 years old, has been followed by a neurologist for many years due to severe spastic paraparesis of unknown etiology.
In January 2024, the patient recovered from an influenza viral infection with pneumonia. Examined by an infectious disease specialist in February 2024 – physical therapy via home care services was advised until admission for inpatient treatment.
**Current complaints:** Presenting for an initial examination due to an infiltrate in the left lung described on a chest X-ray (Nov 13, 2025).
- reports coughing in a supine position (in the evening when lying down), reports a sensation of shortness of breath while speaking. Reports significant weight loss over the past 2 years. Febrile in June and September up to 38°C, lasting 3-4 days, with a productive cough during those episodes. Denies chest pain. Denies "wheezing in the chest." Leg swelling occurred. Denies night sweats and excessive sweating. Reports constantly elevated CRP (does not provide laboratory findings).
**Physiological functions and Habits:** appetite fair, weight loss over the past 2 years. Diaper in place. Bowel movements regular, without pathological admixtures. Urination without burning. Former smoker, quit 30 years ago, 20 cigarettes/day for about 20 years. Does not drink alcohol.
**Medications:** does not take chronic therapy, Brufen as needed.
**Drug allergies:** [None listed]

**Status:**
Of slender build and nutritional status, afebrile, anicteric, eupneic, conscious, contactable, oriented in time and space, poorly mobile, in a wheelchair at the time of examination. Skin and visible mucous membranes well-perfused. Jugular veins normally filled. Heart action regular, sounds clear, no murmurs heard. Diminished breath sounds over the lungs posterobasally, bilaterally. Abdomen soft, painless on palpation, peristalsis audible. Lumbar succussion painless bilaterally. Limbs symmetrical, discrete edema of the lower legs.

**Findings:**
**Date of finding: Jan 30, 2024, 12:43**
**Chest X-ray, sitting:**
On the lung parenchyma available for analysis, no certain signs of fresh inflammatory changes are seen.
Hilovascular markings of normal distribution - calcified mediastinal lymph nodes.
Cardiac silhouette slightly enlarged.
Diaphragmatic domes appropriately shaped, costophrenic (f.c.) sinuses obscured – pleuropulmonary adhesive changes.

**Nov 13, 2025. Follow-up chest X-ray, AP sitting:**
An X-ray from Oct 29, 2025, is available for comparison.
Inhomogeneous opacity is still visible on the left in summation with the cardiac shadow caudohilarly and left basally, with reduced translucency of the left pulmonary base; the medial, underlying part of the left dome is partially unclearly delineated with the left lateral costophrenic (f.c.) sinus obscured, so a small amount of pleural effusion cannot be ruled out. Band-like opacities right laterobasally – differential diagnosis: fibrotic changes. Calcified shadow in the projection of the left hilum, 1.6 cm in diameter – differential diagnosis: calcified lymph node. More intense shadow in the middle pulmonary field on the left peripherally, 1.1 cm in diameter – differential diagnosis: pleural plaque.
The right hemidiaphragm is flattened, the right lateral costophrenic (f.c.) sinus is shallower. Hila are of appropriate terminal distribution, and the caudal distribution of the left hilum is partially summed with the described opacity. Cardiac silhouette size is within physiological proportions.
In the follow-up interval, the finding is without significant changes. According to the clinical presentation, further workup is advised (pulmonologist examination and MSCT of the thorax).

**Oct 28, 2025.** E 5.06, Hb 136, MCV 82.9, T 360, L 5.6, seg 69.5%, ly 17%, mono 10.5%, bazo 1.2%, GUK 4.9, urea 6.4, creatinine 67, ALT 11, AST 17, GGT 23, ALP 66, K 5.1, Na 140, Fe 11.

**Conclusion:**
It is advised to perform laboratory tests (CBC, differential blood count, CRP, LDH, Na, K, Cl, Ca, urea, creatinine, AST, ALT, GGT, NT-proBNP, urine). Microbiological analysis of sputum is advised (culture and sensitivity, Koch's bacillus, fungi). Pulmonology follow-up with findings.

**Diagnoses**
J44.0 Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with acute lower respiratory infection

**History:**
Reason for visit: pulmonologist follow-up, last exam on Nov 13, 2024.
Personal history and previous illnesses: Patient between 70 and 75 years old is being treated for type 2 diabetes mellitus, BPH, hypothyroidism (total thyroidectomy performed due to medullary thyroid carcinoma). Surgery of the LS spine in 2016. In 2017, had surgery of the right ICA. In 2021, a double aortocoronary bypass was performed (LIMA-LAD, VSM-RCA). Treated for asthma/COPD? for the last 4 years. According to ENT, has chronic laryngitis. Last pulmonology follow-up was in 11/2024; use of Alvesco instead of Foster was advised.
Current complaints: comes for a pulmonologist follow-up, last exam on Nov 13, 2024. Denies difficulties with breathing. Denies feeling short of breath. States that he occasionally has changes in voice tone, occasionally "loses" his voice. No cough. Denies chest pain. Previously had "wheezing in the chest," no longer "wheezes" for the past year. Occasionally clear nasal secretion.
Epidemiological history: recovered from COVID-19 in Feb 2021—mild clinical picture, and in 2022—since then has had issues with hoarseness.
Functions and habits: good appetite, stable body weight for the last 6 months, urinates 2-3x at night. Stools are normal, without pathological admixtures. Has not smoked for 35 years, smoked for 20 years, 20 cigarettes/day. Drinks 2-3 dl of wine daily.
Vaccinations: not vaccinated against COVID-19 and flu.
Current therapy: Alvesco 1x1 puff, Euthyrox 100 mcg/day, Aspirin 100 mg 1x1 tab, Dalneva 4/5 mg 2x1 tab, Controloc 1x1 tab, Duster Duo 1x1 caps, Rosuvastatin 1x1 tab in the evening, Siofor 850 mg 2x1 tab, Zaldiar 1 tab/day, Melart, three types of eye drops.
Allergies: denies drug allergies.

**Status:**
Average musculoskeletal build and nutritional status, mobile, conscious, eupneic, anicteric, afebrile. Postoperative scar on the neck. Impression of normal perfusion of the skin and visible mucous membranes. Neck veins not distended, no palpable lymph nodes in the neck. Status post median thoracotomy. Lungs: diminished breath sounds posterobasally on the right compared to the left side. Heart: rhythmic action, clear tones, no murmurs heard. Abdomen: soft, non-tender on palpation, audible peristalsis. LS region non-tender bilaterally. Limbs: status post left saphenectomy, pitting edema of the lower legs. Mild clubbing of the fingers.

**Findings:**
Date of finding: Apr 14, 2025, 11:38

Nov 13: FENO: 55 ppb—elevated value.
Nov 13, 2024: Spirometry with BDT: FVC 95.5-93.5, FEV1 96.1-104.1, TI 77.52-82.46, MEF50: 95.6-127.9—values within reference ranges, BDT negative, but with improvement at the level of small airways after bronchodilation.
Dec 9, 2025: Spirometry with BDT: FVC 88-91% (3.44L), FEV1 89-96% (2.66L), TI 0.77-0.81, PEF 110-99%, FEF50 85-122% DLCO 86%, KCO 85%, VA 100%
Mar 6, 2025: Chest X-ray (PA and lateral profile): the pulmonary parenchyma available for analysis shows no convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes. An oval shadow is visible basally on the left, diff. dg. nipple shadow. In case of clinical necessity, perform an X-ray with marked nipples. Hila are vascular, with normal terminal branching. The left diaphragmatic dome and corresponding costophrenic sinus are clear. The right diaphragmatic dome is positioned higher, lateral costophrenic sinus is shallower. Heart shadow size within physiological proportions. Status post sternotomy.

**Conclusion:**
I advise continuing the use of Alvesco now at a double dose and other chronic therapy as before. Plan a follow-up in 3 months with new findings: spirometry with Ventolin test, DLCO, FENO, chest X-ray (PA, lateral with marking of the nipples), lab findings (CBC, WBC differential, CRP, urea, creatinine, AST, ALT, GGT, Na, K, Cl, Ca, LDH, NT-proBNP, urine). ENT specialist examination and cardiologist examination are advised. Bring the inhaler to the follow-up (question of inhalation technique). Earlier follow-up according to clinical indication.

In May 2025, a left-sided nephroureterectomy was performed due to a neoplasm of the left ureter and renal pelvis.
On December 8, 2025, examined by a rheumatologist due to pain and swelling of the hands, more on the right; managed under the diagnosis of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis.
Current complaints: Referred to a pulmonologist due to MSCT thorax findings; a urological surgical procedure, urinary bladder polypectomy, is planned.
One month ago had a cough and a cold, was afebrile. Feels well now, does not cough, denies chest pain, denies shortness of breath.
Current therapy: Decortin (since Dec 8) 10 mg for 5 days, 7.5 mg for 7 days and continue 5 mg until follow-up, Methotrexate 10 mg once weekly (4 tablets of 2.5 mg), Folbella 5 mg 1x1 tablet every day except on the day of methotrexate intake for two weeks, and after that 1x1 tablet the day after methotrexate intake, D vital 25,000 I.U. once monthly.
Functions and habits: Smoked up to 10 cigarettes a day for the past 30 years, quit 1.5 months ago, does not consume alcohol.
Allergies: Denies drug allergies.

Status:
Auscultation: bilateral normal breath sounds, SpO2 93%
Findings:
Date of finding: Nov 17, 2025, 19:52
Finding and opinion
May 3, 2024, TUR of the urinary bladder, PHD: CARCINOMA PAPILLARE UROTHELIALE NON INVASIVUM (low grade - WHO)
MSCT of the thorax, i.v. contrast:
Perifissural nodule of the small interlobium size 5 mm. Left upper and lower lobe and right basal linear fibrous changes reaching the pleura. No significantly enlarged lymph nodes or tumor masses seen in the mediastinum and hila. Tracheobronchial tree is in an appropriate position and lumen width. Left pleural effusion 1.8 cm wide. Visualized bones are without pathological remodeling typical for secondary deposits.
MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, with i.v. contrast and urography: May 21, 2025, Nephroureterectomia lat. sin. Pronounced left-sided scoliosis of the lumbar spine. Scarring changes visible to the right in the left renal fossa. Right perirenal space is free. Right kidney is of appropriate position and size, maintained corticomedullary borders. In the delayed phase, contrast urine is visible in the collecting system of the right kidney. Right ureter is of normal lumen width, followed to the junction with the urinary bladder with appropriate density of surrounding fatty tissue. Multiple polypoid thickenings of the dorsal wall of the urinary bladder size 4 to 5 mm are visible. No significantly enlarged lymph nodes or free fluid seen in the scanned area.
Apart from a cyst of the liver parenchyma, other parenchymal organs of the upper abdomen are of normal morphology. Visualized bones are without pathological remodeling typical for secondary deposits.

Lung Ultrasound: left small anechoic zone up to one intercostal space in height corresponding to pleural effusion.

Conclusion:
Perform follow-up Chest X-ray in one month and follow-up MSCT of the thorax in 12 months.
Follow-up in the pulmonology clinic with the MSCT finding, earlier if necessary.

label: CLEAN_REPORT

report_content:
Diagnoses
J45 Asthma

History:
Reason for visit: preoperative examination - left URS is planned
Personal history and previous illnesses: Female patient aged between 60 and 65 years is being treated for asthma and osteoporosis. In 03/2025, underwent surgery for a diaphragmatic hernia. Last pulmonologist check-up in 08/2025 - advised to continue current inhalation therapy, follow-up in 3 months.
Current complaints: States that symptoms are stationary - tolerates major physical exertion poorly, dyspneic when walking uphill. Coughs every morning, expectorates thick yellowish secretion. No wheezing in the chest. Denies stenocardia and other complaints.

Current therapy: Relvar 184/22 mcg 1x1 inhalation, Ventolin PRN, Nixar 20 mg 1x1 tab, Melarth 10 mg 1x1 tab, D-Vital, D3 Dekristol, Zaldiar tab. PRN

Functions and habits: Smokes up to 20 cig/day for about 30 years. Occasional alcohol consumption.
Appetite normal, no significant fluctuations in body weight. Stool and urination normal.
Allergies: Denies drug allergies.

Status:
Conscious, normal contact, oriented to time and space, medium musculoskeletal build, mobile, eupneic, afebrile, anicteric. Skin and visible mucous membranes normally perfused. Skin without rashes. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. Jugular veins not distended, no vascular bruits over the carotids. Tongue moist, uncoated.
Heart: rhythmic action, heart sounds muffled, no murmurs heard. Pulse 85/min.
Lungs: on auscultation prolonged expiration with rare wheezes on the right, SpO2 96%
Extremities: symmetrical, no edema. Palpable peripheral pulsations.

Findings:
12/03/2025 - Chest X-ray
Bilateral diffuse prominent hilovascular and partly interstitial pulmonary markings are visible. In the middle lung field on the right, a more intense round shadow of 0.9 cm is seen - diff. dg. pleural plaque.
12/08/2025 lab: Hb 139 RBC (E) 4.54 WBC (Leuk) 7.7 Plt (Trc) 231 Eosinophils 0.49 Glucose (GUK), urea, creatinine, transaminases - NORMAL, CRP 9.1, Urine - leukocyte esterase 2+, erythrocytes mass, leukocytes many, bacteria some
IgE - none
12/09 SPIROMETRY with Ventolin test: FVC 58% (56%), FEV1 46% (45%) Tiffeneau 80% (80%) PEF 48% (50%) PEF25 42% (35%) PEF 50 20% (20%) - obstructive-restrictive impairment of a severe degree
DLCO 71%
KCO 99%

Conclusion:
Discontinue Relvar from therapy and introduce Timbow 2x2 inhalations per day, along with Ventolin PRN and other current therapy.
Take Medrol 32 mg for the next 5 days.
Perform microbiological analysis of sputum.
Follow-up in the pulmonology outpatient clinic in 3 months with spirometry with Ventolin test, DLCO, FeNO, and a new chest X-ray (PA + L profile), laboratory blood findings (CBC, DC, CRP, total IgE).
